metadata.summary: Prevent N+1 queries in Django with select_related, prefetch_related, custom Prefetch, only/defer, and assertNumQueries regression locks.

content.title: Django Query Optimization and N+1 Prevention
content.summary: N+1 queries are the most common performance leak in Django. A single for loop over a queryset without select_related becomes 1 + N database calls. This methodology covers select_related, prefetch_related, custom Prefetch objects, only/defer for large fields, and assertNumQueries to lock query counts as regression tests.

why.p: N+1 is invisible until you exceed 100 rows. A view that loads 10 orders and accesses order.user.email issues 11 queries. With 1000 orders it issues 1001 queries — the difference between 200ms and 20s response times at typical Django ORM overhead. select_related adds a JOIN (same single-query cost); prefetch_related runs exactly 2 queries regardless of result set size (much cheaper than 1+N). Django Debug Toolbar makes the problem visible during development; nplusone autodetects it in tests.

Indexes on frequently filtered fields are the other half: a query that correctly uses select_related but filters on an unindexed column does a full table scan. Both must be addressed together. assertNumQueries in tests locks the query count, catching accidental N+1 reintroduction in code review.

list.item: Any Django view that iterates over a queryset and accesses related objects.
List API endpoints returning more than 10 rows — N+1 impact grows linearly with row count.
Admin list views that display FK fields (automatic N+1 from list_display).
Celery tasks that process batches of model instances with relationships.
Any endpoint where performance targets matter (SLA, dashboard, mobile API).
Single-object detail views accessing only direct fields — no relationship traversal, no N+1 risk.
Management commands processing one row at a time where simplicity beats efficiency.
Throwaway scripts where correctness matters more than speed.

section.name: select-related-prefetch
section.p: The fundamental rule: select_related for ForeignKey / OneToOneField (single SQL JOIN), prefetch_related for ManyToManyField / reverse ForeignKey / GenericForeignKey (2 separate SQL queries). Apply at the selector level so views do not need to worry about it.

rule.statement: Every QuerySet that will be iterated MUST declare select_related() for every ForeignKey/OneToOne field accessed in the loop body, and prefetch_related() for every ManyToMany or reverse FK accessed in the loop body, BEFORE the loop. Do not add these in the view — put them in selector/manager methods so the optimization is centrally owned and reused. One query per view is impossible; targets: list endpoints ≤5 queries, detail endpoints ≤10 queries.
rule.rationale: select_related adds a JOIN — same query, zero additional round trips. prefetch_related issues exactly 2 queries regardless of result size. Centralizing in selectors means every caller (views, admin, Celery, management commands) gets the optimization automatically. Targets give a concrete, testable threshold for code review.

example.title: N+1 queries — 1 + N calls for N orders
example.code: # PROBLEM: N+1 queries (1 query for orders + 1 per order for user) def get_orders_bad(): orders = Order.objects.all() for order in orders: print(order.user.email) # Query per order! print(order.shipping_address) # Another query per order! return orders
example.note: With 500 orders this issues 1001 queries. Debug Toolbar shows the spike.

example.title: select_related eliminates FK N+1

section.name: query-counting
section.p: Django Debug Toolbar shows query counts in the browser during development. nplusone middleware autodetects N+1 in test runs. assertNumQueries locks the count in regression tests so accidental N+1 reintroduction is caught at CI.

rule.statement: Every hot endpoint (list views, dashboard, high-traffic API) MUST have an assertNumQueries regression test that locks the exact query count. Use Django Debug Toolbar (dev) to profile new views. Enable nplusone middleware in development settings to autodetect N+1 without manual inspection. Install django-silk for production profiling of top 10 slow requests.
rule.rationale: assertNumQueries is a zero-cost regression lock: it fails immediately when someone adds an N+1 and the CI catches it before merge. Without it, N+1 regressions are invisible until load testing. Debug Toolbar is the fastest way to see the problem during development — it shows the full query list with duplicates highlighted.

example.title: assertNumQueries regression test
example.code:
# tests/test_views.py import pytest from django.test import TestCase
class TestOrderListView(TestCase): def test_order_list_query_count(self): """Lock query count to prevent N+1 regression.""" # Create 10 orders with users and items users = [User.objects.create_user(username=f"user{i}") for i in range(10)] for i, user in enumerate(users): order = Order.objects.create(user=user) OrderItem.objects.create(order=order, quantity=1)
# Baseline: 1 query for orders + 1 for users + 1 for items = 3 total with self.assertNumQueries(3): response = self.client.get("/api/orders/") self.assertEqual(response.status_code, 200)
# nplusone middleware in dev settings: # MIDDLEWARE += ["nplusone.ext.django.NPlusOneMiddleware"] # NPLUSONE_RAISE = True # Raise exception on N+1 detection in tests

example.title: Selector pattern centralizing query optimization

section.name: bulk-operations
section.p: Bulk creates and updates reduce round trips for batch processing. bulk_create avoids the 1 INSERT per object pattern. update() on querysets avoids the 1 UPDATE per object pattern. F() expressions handle counter updates atomically without read-modify-write races.

rule.statement: Use bulk_create() when creating multiple model instances in a loop. Use QuerySet.update() or F() expressions when updating the same field on multiple rows. Never iterate with .save() for batch creates or counter increments. Set batch_size in bulk_create to control memory for very large batches.
rule.rationale: bulk_create issues a single INSERT with multiple values rows — O(1) queries instead of O(N). QuerySet.update() issues a single UPDATE WHERE — O(1) vs O(N). F() expressions are evaluated server-side and are safe from race conditions under concurrent writes.

example.title: Bulk operations for batch processing

section.name: indexes
section.p: Indexes on frequently filtered or ordered fields turn full table scans into index lookups. Django supports single-column and composite indexes. CheckConstraints and UniqueConstraints can also carry partial indexes. Database indexes are the single biggest knob after N+1 elimination.

rule.statement: Add database indexes (models.Index) to every field used in: (1) .filter() clauses in hot paths; (2) .order_by() clauses on large tables; (3) foreign key fields not covered by Django's auto-index. Add composite indexes for common multi-field filter patterns (e.g., is_active + created_at). Verify index usage with EXPLAIN ANALYZE in development.
rule.rationale: A query on an unindexed field does a sequential scan — O(rows) cost. An index lookup is O(log N). For a 100K-row table the difference is 10,000x. Django's default auto-index covers only the primary key and explicit unique fields; application-specific filter patterns require explicit model indexes.

example.title: Model with proper indexes
example.code:
from django.db import models from django.db.models import CheckConstraint, Q, UniqueConstraint from django.core.validators import MinValueValidator
class Product(models.Model): """Product model with proper validation, constraints, and indexes."""
name = models.CharField(max_length=200) slug = models.SlugField(max_length=200, unique=True) description = models.TextField(blank=True) price = models.DecimalField( max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, validators=[MinValueValidator(0)] ) stock = models.PositiveIntegerField(default=0) is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True) created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True) updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
class Meta: ordering = ["-created_at"] verbose_name = "Product" verbose_name_plural = "Products" indexes = [ models.Index(fields=["slug"]), # unique implies index, but explicit is clearer models.Index(fields=["is_active", "-created_at"]), # composite: common filter+sort ] constraints = [ CheckConstraint( check=Q(price__gte=0), name="product_price_non_negative" ), CheckConstraint( check=Q(stock__gte=0), name="product_stock_non_negative" ), ]
def __str__(self) -> str: return self.name
def clean(self) -> None: """Model-level validation beyond field validators.""" super().clean() if self.price and self.price > 1000000: from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError raise ValidationError({"price": "Price cannot exceed 1,000,000"})
@property def is_in_stock(self) -> bool: """Check if product is in stock and available.""" return self.stock > 0 and self.is_active

section.name: gotchas
section.p: Common pitfalls agents encounter with Django query optimization.

antipattern.description: Using select_related on a nullable ForeignKey and iterating without checking None.
antipattern.reason: select_related with a NULL FK produces an outer join and correctly returns None for the related object. Agents sometimes add .exclude(fk__isnull=True) to avoid None checks, which changes the result set semantics.

antipattern.description: Using prefetch_related but then filtering the related queryset in Python inside the loop.
antipattern.reason: Filtering in Python after prefetch breaks the optimization — the filter re-issues queries. Use Prefetch(queryset=...) with the filter applied to the queryset argument instead.

antipattern.description: Adding select_related to every queryset including those that never access the related object.
antipattern.reason: Unnecessary JOINs add CPU and memory cost. Profile first with Debug Toolbar, add select_related only where the related field is actually accessed.

antipattern.description: prefetch_related on a queryset that has already been evaluated (e.g., list(queryset)).
antipattern.reason: prefetch_related works at queryset evaluation time. Calling it after list() has no effect. Use prefetch_related_objects() for already-evaluated querysets.

checklist.check: Every queryset iterating FK access uses select_related()
Every queryset iterating M2M or reverse FK uses prefetch_related()
Hot endpoints have assertNumQueries regression tests
Debug Toolbar installed in development settings
nplusone middleware active in test settings (NPLUSONE_RAISE=True)
Batch creates use bulk_create() not loops with .save()
Counter increments use F() expressions not read-modify-write
Composite indexes added for common filter+sort patterns
Admin list_display FK fields covered by select_related in get_queryset()
